section.title: Final Instructions and Greetings
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes with practical travel plans, the sending of coworkers, provision for Zenas and Apollos, and a final greeting. Yet even here the letter's great theme surfaces one last time: our people must learn to devote themselves to good works, and the closing blessing is simply grace. The mundane and the gospel are woven together.
section.thesis: Even the ordinary logistics of ministry, comings and goings, provision, and greetings, are the setting for a life devoted to fruitful good works, all held together and blessed by the grace that runs through the whole letter.
section.frameNote: Two movements: final instructions about travel, coworkers, and provision, capped by the recurring call to devote our lives to good works so we are not unfruitful; then the closing greetings and the benediction of grace to all.

units.label: Travel plans and provision
units.note: learn to do good and be fruitful

verses.text: As soon as I send Artemas or Tychicus to you, {{spoudazo|make every effort}} to come to me at Nicopolis, because I have decided to winter there.
Do your best to equip Zenas the lawyer and Apollos, so that they will have everything they need.
And our people must also learn to devote themselves to {{kalaerga|good works}} in order to meet the pressing needs of others, so that they will not be unfruitful.
All who are with me send you greetings. Greet those who love us in the faith. {{charis|Grace}} be with all of you.

spoudazo.gloss: To make every effort, to be diligent, to hasten. It conveys eager, purposeful haste. Paul uses it for Titus coming to Nicopolis and, closely related, for the diligent equipping of Zenas and Apollos. Even ordinary ministry arrangements are to be pursued with earnest care, not casual indifference.

kalaerga.gloss: Good works, literally beautiful or excellent deeds. The phrase is a drumbeat through Titus, and here believers must learn to devote themselves to such works to meet urgent needs. Good works are not optional extras but the ordinary fruit of grace, so that the redeemed life is a useful, fruitful one.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's undeserved favour in Christ. The letter opened with grace, verse 1:4, and now closes with it: grace be with all of you. Grace both frames and fills the letter, the source of salvation in chapters 2 and 3 and the final word of blessing over the whole church.

basic.context: Paul wraps up the letter with practical matters: he will send Artemas or Tychicus so Titus can join him at Nicopolis for the winter, and Titus should generously supply Zenas and Apollos for their journey. Then, one more time, Paul urges God's people to devote themselves to good works so their lives bear fruit. He closes with greetings and a blessing of grace.
basic.hermeneutics: These closing verses are real history, naming real people and places, which reminds us the letter is grounded in actual ministry. Even so, Paul cannot sign off without returning to his great theme of good works, showing that doctrine is meant to land in everyday, useful service. The final word, grace, echoes the letter's opening.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul urges Titus to spoudazo, to make every effort, to come, and to do his best to equip the travellers. Believers must learn to devote themselves to kala erga, good works, literally beautiful deeds, so they are not unfruitful. The letter ends as it began, with charis, grace, be with all.
basic.typology: The naming of ordinary coworkers and the call to meet pressing needs reflect the body of Christ at work, each member supplying what others lack, a living picture of the mutual care that marks God's people in every age.
basic.christ: Though Christ is not named in these final verses, the grace that blesses the church is His grace, the grace whose appearing in Jesus the whole letter has celebrated. The good works urged here flow from the people He redeemed and purified for Himself.
basic.god: God's grace is the first and last word over His people. He cares about practical provision, fruitful lives, and the bonds of love among believers, showing that He is Lord of the ordinary as well as the eternal.
basic.crossReferences: Second Timothy 4 shows Paul similarly arranging coworkers and travel. Acts 18 introduces Apollos, an eloquent teacher of the Scriptures. And Philippians 4 shows the same care to meet one another's pressing needs.
basic.oneStory: The letter ends where it began, in grace, framing all its instruction. Ordinary logistics become the arena for a fruitful, good-works life among a people saved by grace. It is a small window on the whole story: a redeemed community, cared for and blessed by grace, living usefully in the world until Christ appears.

context.literary: A typical Pauline letter closing: travel plans, personal names, a final exhortation, greetings, and a benediction. Yet the placement of the good-works charge in verse 14, just before the farewell, shows Paul deliberately sounding his central theme one last time.
context.historical: Nicopolis, likely the city in Epirus on the Adriatic coast, was a strategic wintering base. Tychicus was a trusted Pauline courier, and Apollos the eloquent Alexandrian teacher known from Acts. Zenas the lawyer and Apollos were probably the bearers of this very letter, needing the churches' support for their journey.
context.canonical: The closing mirrors the endings of Paul's other letters, especially the Pastorals, binding Titus into the network of early Christian mission and mutual support, and sealing the letter with the grace that is the New Testament's characteristic blessing.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To settle practical ministry arrangements, ensure the support of travelling gospel workers, and press home once more that the whole church, not just leaders, must devote itself to fruitful good works, all under the blessing of grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The travel plans and names are descriptive of a particular moment. The call in verse 14 to learn to devote ourselves to good works and to meet pressing needs is prescriptive and enduring, as is the pattern of generously supporting gospel workers.

debates.question: Why does Paul repeat the call to good works even in the farewell?

views.label: Good works are the letter's central practical burden
views.case: From 1:16 to 3:14 the theme recurs relentlessly; ending on it shows Paul wants the church defined by fruitful, useful lives, not empty profession.

views.label: To guard grace from being misheard as passivity
views.case: Having grounded everything in grace and mercy, Paul insists that grace produces action, so no one mistakes free salvation for a fruitless faith.

debates.bearing: Either way, the letter refuses to divorce grace from good works: those saved by grace must learn to live usefully and fruitfully.

study.originalLanguages: Twice Paul presses diligence: Titus is to spoudazo, to make every effort, to reach Nicopolis, and to do his best to equip Zenas and Apollos, so that even logistics carry an earnest, purposeful energy. The climactic exhortation lands on kala erga, good works, literally beautiful or excellent deeds, a phrase that has drummed through the whole letter; God's people must learn to devote themselves to such works to meet pressing needs, so that they are not unfruitful. Then the letter closes with the word it opened on, charis, grace: grace be with all of you, wrapping the entire epistle in the favour of God.

typology.type: The body supplying one another's needs
typology.fulfillment: A picture of the church as Christ's body, each member equipping and supporting the others, so that gospel work goes forward and no need is left unmet.

study.christ: Christ is not named in these closing lines, yet He fills them. The grace that blesses the whole church is the grace of the Lord Jesus, the grace whose appearing to save and to train has been the letter's great song. The beautiful good works Paul commands are the fruit of the very people Christ gave Himself to redeem and purify, so that even this quiet farewell rests entirely on His finished work and continuing favour.
study.god: God is the giver of grace, and grace is His first and final word over His people. He is concerned with the practical, provision for travellers, the meeting of pressing needs, the ordinary comings and goings of ministry, as well as with the eternal. He wills that His people be fruitful, not idle, and He blesses the whole community, not merely its leaders, with His grace.

crossReferences.note: Paul making similar arrangements for coworkers and travel, showing the human texture of gospel ministry.
Apollos, the eloquent, Scripture-mighty teacher, here to be supplied for his journey.
God supplies every need, the counterpart to the church meeting one another's pressing needs.
Created in Christ for good works, the fruitfulness Paul urges one last time in verse 14.

study.oneStory: The letter ends where it began, in grace, and that frame says everything: a people saved by grace are sent to live fruitful, good-works lives in the ordinary world, caring for one another's needs until Christ appears. These closing logistics are a small picture of the whole story, a redeemed community, blessed and upheld by grace, getting on with useful love in the world while it waits for the blessed hope.

sermon.title: Ordinary People and Everyday Events
sermon.why: The closing message of Begg's series A Study in Titus, expounding Titus 3:12-15, shows how the letter's grand gospel themes come to rest in ordinary names, travel plans, and the call to fruitful good works, sealed by grace. A fitting, orthodox treatment of this final movement.

quiz.q: Why does Paul want Titus to come to Nicopolis?

options.text: Paul has decided to winter there and sends a replacement so Titus can join him.
options.note: Verse 12 explains the plan to send Artemas or Tychicus and meet at Nicopolis.

options.text: To abandon the churches in Crete for good.
options.note: Paul sends a replacement first; the work in Crete is not abandoned.

options.text: Because Titus was in trouble and needed rescue.
options.note: The text gives ministry coordination, not rescue, as the reason.

options.text: To put Titus on trial.
options.note: There is no hint of a trial; it is a ministry rendezvous.

quiz.q: What does Paul ask concerning Zenas and Apollos?

options.text: That Titus diligently equip them so they lack nothing for their journey.
options.note: Verse 13 calls for generous provision of the travelling workers.

options.text: That Titus turn them away as false teachers.
options.note: They are trusted coworkers to be supported, not opposed.

options.text: That Titus ignore their needs.
options.note: The opposite; he is to see they have everything they need.

options.text: That Titus report them to the authorities.
options.note: Paul asks for their support, not their exposure.

quiz.q: What must God's people learn to do, according to verse 14?

options.text: Devote themselves to good works to meet pressing needs, so as not to be unfruitful.
options.note: Verse 14 sounds the letter's theme of fruitful good works once more.

options.text: Focus only on their own comfort.
options.note: The call is to meet others' pressing needs, not self-comfort.

options.text: Withdraw from all practical service.
options.note: Paul urges active, need-meeting good works, not withdrawal.

options.text: Earn salvation through their works.
options.note: Good works flow from grace already received, not to earn salvation.

quiz.q: How does the letter to Titus close?

options.text: With greetings and the blessing, grace be with all of you.
options.note: Verse 15 ends with mutual greetings and the benediction of grace.

options.text: With a stern final warning and no blessing.
options.note: There are warnings earlier, but the letter closes with grace.

options.text: With a long list of Paul's achievements.
options.note: The close is greetings and grace, not self-commendation.

options.text: With no ending at all.
options.note: It ends with a clear greeting and benediction of grace.

quiz.q: What does it show that the letter opens and closes with grace?

options.text: Grace frames and fills the whole letter, the source and seal of the Christian life.
options.note: Grace in 1:4 and 3:15 brackets everything Paul teaches.

options.text: That grace is only a polite formality.
options.note: Grace is the letter's living theme, not mere politeness.

options.text: That good works have replaced grace.
options.note: Good works flow from grace; they do not replace it.

options.text: That the ending is unrelated to the rest.
options.note: The closing grace deliberately echoes the letter's opening and heart.

questions: Paul weaves the gospel into travel plans and provision for coworkers. How might God be inviting you to see your ordinary logistics and errands as arenas for serving Him?
Believers are to learn to devote themselves to good works. What is one pressing need around you that you could actually help meet this week?
Paul warns against being unfruitful. Where do you sense your faith has grown passive, and what would fruitfulness look like there?
The church supported Zenas and Apollos so they lacked nothing. Who are the gospel workers you could be generously supporting right now?
The letter begins and ends with grace. How would resting more deeply in God's grace change the way you serve, give, and relate to others?

translationNotes.note: Nicopolis most likely refers to the city in Epirus on the Adriatic coast, a natural base from which to winter and coordinate onward mission.
Zenas is called a lawyer, either an expert in Jewish law or a Roman jurist; he and Apollos were probably carrying this letter and needed provision for the road.
Some manuscripts (BYZ and TR) add Amen at the end, as the BSB footnote notes; the earliest witnesses close simply with grace be with all of you.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Titus 3:12-15. No em or en dashes appear in this passage.
